text: The surgeon removes omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, demonstrating a clear separation between the omentum and the abdominal wall tissue.
The surgeon visually estimates the ventral incisional hernia to be approximately two centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, revealing herniated preperitoneal adipose tissue protruding through the defect.
The surgeon exposes the full hernia defect and estimates its size, preparing to use a 6.4 cm piece of mesh. The preperitoneal pocket is extended circumferentially to accommodate the mesh placement.
The surgeon separates the peritoneal lining from the posterior rectus fascia, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the hernia repair.
The fascial layer is prepared to incorporate the mesh, ensuring a stronger and more reliable integration compared to fixation on the outside of the peritoneal layer.
The surgeon identifies the hernia sac as a small protrusion on the peritoneal layer, with the hernia contents visibly inverted back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon receives the suture material and a piece of mesh from the assistant, preparing to place the mesh into the preperitoneal pocket that has been extended circumferentially.
The surgeon closes the defect primarily using a running technique with an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the edges of the tissue are approximated securely.

The surgeon adds four additional throws to the barbed suture to maintain tension, then revisits the suture line, ending at the center of the hernia. The final pass of the suture is incorporated into the mesh fixation.
The surgeon circumferentially sutures the mesh to the posterior fascial wall using an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the mesh remains in apposition during the healing process.
The herniated contents are visibly reduced back into the abdominal space, demonstrating the effectiveness of the operative approach.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to the robust rectus fascia using sutures, ensuring stability and preventing migration of the mesh.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal space over the mesh, effectively concealing it from the intestine to prevent adhesions and potential bowel obstructions.
The surgeon removes all suture material from the abdomen, marking the completion of the operation.
